The spectra are emitted from the gas downstream of different shock fronts which are at work on the edges of the emitting clouds. A head-on-back shock appears when the wind from the SB stars reaches the internal edge of the SB debris moving outwards. A head-on shock is created by collision of the debris with the ISM clouds. Line ratios in both cases are calculated by the coupled effect of shock and photoionization from the SB.

sources.summary: The paper revisits HG031203 with VLT spectra, deriving extinction, gas density, temperature, metallicity, star-formation rate, redshift, and its comparison to other GRB-supernova host galaxies.

quotes.quote: We revisit the host galaxy of GRB031203 using a set of spectra obtained with VLT. Assuming a Galactic color excess E(B-V) = 0.72 (mag) in the direction of the burst, we derive an internal extinction of E(B-V)HG 0.4 (mag) . After correcting for reddening, we find an electronic density of 156 21(cm^ 3 ) and a temperature of 12443 95 (K) . With an ISM dominated by photo-ionization, we estimate a metallicity of 12+Log [ O/H ] =8.12 0.04 and a star formation rate (SFR) of 12.3 2.1 (M_ o yr^ 1 ) . This galaxy does not host a clearly detectable population of WR stars.

From the five strongest emission features of each spectra we derive a redshift z=0.10536 0.00001 which corresponds to a luminosity distance of 473 (Mpc) when standard cosmology is used (H_ o =72 ( Km s^ 1 Mpc^ 1 ) , _ m = 0.3 , _ =0.7 ): this makes GRB031203 one of the closest long burst ever observed. Because of the presence of the underlying supernova 2003lw, HG031203 enter the class of galaxies of the local universe ( z 0.1 ) which are known to have hosted a LGRB and a supernova explosion. In this work we compare HG031203 and other GRB-SNe Host galaxies (HG980425, HG030329, HG060218) to "normal" local galaxies of the universe.

sources.summary: The paper flags the host of GRB031203/SN2003lw as a possible AGN-contaminated SN-GRB host with high [O III]/H ratios affecting sample comparisons.

quotes.quote: The ionization source for all the host galaxies in our PTF SN and SN-GRB samples is star formation rather than AGN activity, except potentially for the host of GRB031203/SN2003lw. Thus, the strong line methods that we adopt for metallicity calculations are valid for almost all of the hosts in our sample, so are the H based SFRs for SN-GRB hosts in particular. However, the AGN contamination may render the metallicity and SFR measurements incorrect for the host of GRB031203/SN2003lw. We present our analysis results with and without this object and plot it with a different symbol, in order to assess its impact. See Appendix app:bpt for more details on the classification of all host galaxies, especially why we cannot rule out AGN contamination for the host of GRB031203/SN2003lw.

If GRB031203/SN2003lw with potential AGN contribution is included in the SN-GRB sample, the p value of Peto Peto generalized Wilcoxon test on the distributions of [ N ii ] 6584 /H ratios is 0.210 ( >0.05 ), but that of the Wilcoxon rank sum test on the distributions of [ O iii ] 5007 /H ratios is 0.022 ( <0.05 ). However, if GRB031203/SN2003lw is excluded, the same p values are 0.26 and 0.08 (both >0.05 ), respectively for the [ N ii ] 6584 /H and [ O iii ] 5007 /H ratios. Due to the fact that the GRB031203/SN2003lw host has a high [ O iii ] 5007 /H ratio, and that the Wilcoxon rank sum test is sensitive to the difference in the tails of the distributions, the null result is reliable for the comparison between the [ O iii ] 5007 /H ratios of the SN Ic-bl and SN-GRB hosts excluding potential AGNs.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 031203 host as an actively star-forming z=0.1055 galaxy, deriving strong extinction, low metallicity, roughly solar N/Ne/S/Ar pattern, and a dust-corrected SFR above 11 solar masses per year.

quotes.quote: We present Keck/NIRSPEC near-IR images and Magellan/IMACS optical spectroscopy of the host galaxy of GRB 031203. The host is an actively star-forming galaxy at z=0.1055 0.0001 .

From the hydrogen Balmer lines, we infer an extinction of A_V = 3.62 0.25 or a total reddening E_T(B-V) = 1.17 0.1 toward the sightline to the nebular regions. After correcting for reddening, we perform an emission-line analysis and derive an ISM temperature of T=13400 2000 K and electron density of n_e = 300 3 . These imply a metallicity O/H = -0.72 0.15 dex and a roughly solar abundance pattern for N, Ne, S, and Ar. Integrating , we infer a dust-corrected star formation rate (SFR) of > 11 M_ yr^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats the GRB 031203 host as a radio-detected GRB host whose flatter spectral index may imply free-free emission or a younger stellar population.

quotes.quote: Recently the host of GRB 031203 has also been detected at 5.5 GHz by stanway10 . We assume that the entire flux is due to star formation and not active galactic nucleus (AGN) activity, which is a well-tested hypothesis for GRB hosts see discussion in michalowski08,watson11 . The SFRs derived from our radio data as well as from the ultraviolet (UV) data are presented in Table tab:flux and are shown as a function of redshift on Figure fig:sfrz .

On the other hand, the spectral index of the GRB 031203 host is flatter ^ 2.37 _ 1.39 =-0.53 0.50 . This suggests a higher contribution of free--free emission or synchrotron self-absorption; condon and, hence, younger stellar population bressan02,cannon04,hirashitahunt06,clemens08 .

sources.summary: The paper models optical and mid-infrared lines from the LGRB 031203 host, attributing the mid-IR emission to thin shock-dominated clouds and optical lines to radiation-dominated outflows.

quotes.quote: The modelling of the observed [ SIV ] 10.51 / [ SIII ] 18.71 and [ NeIII ] 10.6 / [ NeII ] 12.81 line ratios from LGRB 031203 host galaxy at z=0.105 shows that the mid-IR lines are emitted from geometrically thin shock dominated filaments which are not reached by the photoionizing flux, while the optical lines are emitted from the radiation dominated outflowing clouds.

The lines in the optical range from GRB 031203 host galaxy were observed and modelled in different ways in the last years (Niino et al and references therein, Contini 2016, etc). We have reproduced the line ratios by the radiation dominated (RD) mN3 model (Table 1), characterized by relatively low O/H and N/H and a relatively high . The model accounts consistently also for the shock. Recently, Watson et al (2016) presented the first mid-IR spectrum of a GRB host (HG031203) by low and high resolution spectroscopy with Spitzer-IRS .

sources.summary: The paper identifies a compact GRB 031203 host at z = 0.1055, reports host-galaxy photometric SN evidence, and detects variable emission coincident with the host.

quotes.quote: A compact galaxy, located at a consistent position, was proposed to be the GRB host galaxy by Pr03 . The redshift was z = 0.1055 0.0001 Pr03,Pr04 , making GRB 031203 the second closest burst after GRB 980425 at z = 0.0085 Ga98 .
Based on photometric monitoring of the host galaxy, several groups have reported evidence for a SN associated with GRB 031203 Be04,Th04,Co04,Ga04 .

Subsequent imaging with ISAAC on the ESO-VLT showed the presence of a varying source coincident with the putative host galaxy of GRB 031203: the total flux had dimmed in the J , H and K filters by a few tenths of a magnitude (see Fig. fg:lc ).

gmw_00000465.name: SDSS J151741.75-004217.6
gmw_00000465.overview: SDSS J151741.75-004217.6 is a low-redshift early-type galaxy with an exceptionally high velocity dispersion for its moderate luminosity [arXiv:1305.5542]. Dynamical models and stellar population analysis indicate that the galaxy possesses a very bottom-heavy initial mass function [arXiv:1305.5542].

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J151741.75-004217.6 as a low-redshift early-type galaxy with exceptional velocity dispersion, bottom-heavy IMF evidence, high mass-to-light ratio, and a possible extremely massive black hole.

quotes.quote: We present orbit-based dynamical models and stellar population analysis of galaxy SDSS J151741.75-004217.6, a low-redshift ( z=0.116 ) early-type galaxy (ETG) which, for its moderate luminosity, has an exceptionally high velocity dispersion. We aim to determine the central black hole mass ( ), the i band stellar mass-to-light ratio ( ), and the low-mass slope of the initial mass function (IMF). Combining constraints from HST imaging and long-slit kinematic data with those from fitting the SDSS spectrum with stellar populations models of varying IMF, we show that this galaxy has a large fraction of low-mass stars, significantly higher than implied even by a Salpeter IMF.

We exclude a Chabrier/Kroupa as well as a unimodal (i.e. single-segment) IMF, while a bimodal (low-mass tapered) shape is consistent with the dynamical constraints. Thereby, our study demonstrates that a very bottom-heavy IMF can exist even in an L^ ETG. We place an upper limit of 10^ 10.5 on , which still leaves open the possibility of an extremely massive BH.

gmw_00000466.name: Galaxy ID 13
gmw_00000466.overview: Galaxy ID 13 is a compact, nearly face-on starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=1.29 [arXiv:1701.07843, arXiv:1512.00246]. It is a member of a nine-member galaxy group and exhibits direct Fe II* emission, which may indicate the presence of galactic outflows [arXiv:1701.07843].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Galaxy ID 13 as a z=1.2902 MUSE HDFS galaxy with direct Fe II* emission, low inclination, starburst-level star formation, and likely galactic outflows.

quotes.quote: The deep IFU observations reveal emission from Fe II * transitions directly detected from one galaxy in the MUSE HDFS. The galaxy has ID 13 in the MUSE catalogue, with coordinates = 22 h 32 m 52.16 s , = -60^ 33 23.92 (J2000) and magnitude I_ 814 = 22.83 0.005 . It is part of a 9-member group at z 1.284 , discussed in , that also includes two AGN and an interacting system with tidal tails. This direct detection of a galaxy with Fe II * emission offers a new opportunity to characterize galactic winds.

From deep MUSE observations of the HDFS, we identify a spatially-resolved galaxy (ID 13) at z=1.2902 that has a low inclination ( i = 33^ ), an orientation that may favor detecting galactic outflows in emission 2014ApJ...794..156R . This galaxy has a star formation rate of , which places it in the starburst category See their Figure 3 2016A&A...591A..49C . Its star formation rate surface density is , well above the threshold for galactic winds 2012ApJ...761...43N, 2014ApJ...784..108B .

sources.summary: The paper describes ID 13 as a compact z 1.28 group member with strong emission and absorption lines, asymmetric central morphology, and eastern low-surface structure.

quotes.quote: ID 13 This galaxy is also a member of the group at z 1.28 . It is rather compact ( 1.5 \ in diameter) in the HST images and its spectrum shows strong \ emission together with prominent \ and \ absorption lines. This galaxy is seen nearly face-on with an inclination of 33 derived with Galfit .

gmw_00000467.name: host of GRB 050724
gmw_00000467.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 050724 is a relatively bright galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.258 [arXiv:1910.14505]. While typically classified as an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1910.14505, arXiv:0912.4142], it exhibits a sub-dominant, faint outer spiral structure [arXiv:1910.14505, arXiv:0909.1804] and has a very low star formation rate [arXiv:1910.14505, arXiv:0912.4142].

sources.summary: The paper reviews the GRB 050724 host as a relatively bright z=0.258 galaxy with modest host extinction, usually classified elliptical but showing faint outer spiral/disk structure and very low star formation.

quotes.quote: The afterglow and its host galaxy are in detail discussed in the literature Berger2005Natur,Gorosabel2006A&A,Prochaska2006ApJ, Malesani2007_050724,Fong2010 . The optical afterglow was located in the outskirts of a relatively bright galaxy ( R 20 mag) at a redshift of z = 0.258 . A global host extinction of A_v^ host 0.4 mag was derived by .

Several authors have classified the host as an elliptical galaxy e.g., Berger2005Natur . Though Malesani2007_050724 found that it shows a faint outer spiral structure, suggesting that is a disk galaxy with a light-dominating central bulge. This conclusion was strengthened by \ images see figure 3 in Fong2010 . Based on optical spectroscopy, the galaxy has a very low star formation rate.

sources.summary: The paper fits the GRB 050724 host SED with an evolved elliptical-galaxy population, finding low extinction and higher stellar mass and luminosity than most long-duration GRB hosts.

quotes.quote: We report optical and near-infrared broad band observations of the short-duration GRB 050724 host galaxy, used to construct its spectral energy distribution (SED). Unlike the hosts of long-duration gamma-ray bursts (GRBs), which show younger stellar populations, the SED of the GRB 050724 host galaxy is optimally fitted with a synthetic elliptical galaxy template based on an evolved stellar population (age 2.6 Gyr). The SED of the host is difficult to reproduce with non-evolving metallicity templates. In contrast, if the short GRB host galaxy metallicity enrichment is considered, the synthetic templates fit the observed SED satisfactorily.

The internal host extinction is low ( A_ v 0.4 mag) so it cannot explain the faintness of the afterglow. This short GRB host galaxy is more massive ( 5 10^ 10 M_ ) and luminous ( 1.1 L^ ) than most of the long-duration GRB hosts.

sources.summary: The host of GRB 050724 is modeled as an elliptical galaxy with sub-dominant weak spiral structure, the highest surface brightness, and a 2.7 kpc burst offset.

quotes.quote: From the galfit analysis we find that three hosts (GRBs 050709, 051221a, and 061006) are best modeled with n 1 , corresponding to an exponential disk profile, while two hosts (GRBs 050509b and 050724) are best modeled with n 3 and 5.6 , respectively, typical of elliptical galaxies. We note that GRB 050724 possibly exhibits weak spiral structure, which may explain the resulting value of n 3 (see Figure  fig:050724 and mcd+07 ), but this putative spiral structure is clearly sub-dominant relative to the elliptical structure.

The smallest effective radius is measured for the host of GRB 060313, while the host of GRB 050509b has the largest effective radius. The median value is r_e 3.5 kpc. We adopt the best-fit values from the radial surface brightness profiles, and plot the resulting distribution, as well as r_e as a function of n , in Figure  fig:re_n . Finally, the effective surface brightness values range from _e 21 to 27 AB mag arcsec ^ 2 . The galaxy with the highest surface brightness is the host of GRB 050724, while the lowest surface brightness is measured for the host of GRB 060121.

Our HST sample includes three short GRBs with strong extended emission (050709, 050724, and 061006), and one GRB (060121) with possible extended emission ( 4.5 significance; dls+06 ). The physical offsets of these bursts are about 3.7, 2.7, 1.3, and 1 kpc, respectively, leading to a mean offset of about 2.2 kpc. The physical offsets of the bursts without extended emission, but with precise afterglow positions (051221, 060313, and 061201) are 2.0, 2.3, and 14.2 or 32.5 kpc, respectively. The two events with no extended emission and with XRT positions (050509b and 051210) have offsets of about 54 12 and 28 23 kpc, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper describes the host of GRB 050724 as an old, non-star-forming elliptical galaxy whose HST morphology has a high Sersic index typical of ellipticals.

quotes.quote: The burst was localized to an elliptical galaxy at z=0.257 with no evidence for on-going star formation ( 0.05 M _ yr ^ 1 ) and a stellar population age of 1 Gyr. The absence of star formation activity and an associated supernova demonstrated a connection with an old stellar population.

We found that three hosts (GRBs 050709, 051221a, and 061006) are best modeled with n 1 , corresponding to an exponential disk profile, while two hosts (GRBs 050509b and 050724) are best modeled with n 3 and 5.6 , respectively, typical of elliptical galaxies. The final three hosts (GRBs 051210, 060121, and 060313) are equally well modeled with a wide range of n values, although n 1 is preferred. Therefore, of the eight short GRB host galaxies with HST observations only two can be robustly classified as elliptical galaxies based on their morphology.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 050724 host as an old galaxy with no significant emission, no recent star formation, and template-fit age and metallicity constraints.

quotes.quote: In the cases of GRB 050509b, GRB 050724, and GRB 050813, we do not detect any significant emission and we employ the SFR calibration of ken98 to place conservative upper limits on the current star formation rate (Table tab:summ ). In the host galaxies of GRB 050509b and GRB 050724, the absence of strong H absorption also indicates that there has been no significant SFR over the past 1 Gyr.
The values compare favorably with other estimates for GRB 050724 gorosabel05 , but our results contradict the young age for GRB 050509b reported by .

gmw_00000468.name: host galaxy of GRB 050709
aliases_local: the host of the short-hard GRB 050709
gmw_00000468.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 050709 is a star-forming dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0510022] at a redshift of z = 0.1606 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509144]. It exhibits an irregular morphology [arXiv:0912.4142], a dominant stellar population under one billion years old [arXiv:astro-ph/0509144], and low metallicity [arXiv:astro-ph/0510022].

sources.original_name: the host of the short-hard GRB 050709
sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 050709 host as a star-forming dwarf galaxy with strong emission lines, reddening-corrected Halpha luminosity, a SFR lower limit, and low metallicity.

quotes.quote: In the case of GRB 050709, there are strong emission lines observed indicating significant ongoing star formation gcn3605,ffc+05 . We have estimated the SFR by first comparing the relative H and H fluxes to measure the reddening along the sightline to the galaxy under the assumption of Case B recombination. We infer a reddening E(B-V) > 0.4 and de-extinct the H emission to derive a luminosity L_ H > 4 10^ 40 ergs/s . We report this as a lower limit because (i) we have applied only a conservative correction to the H flux due to the B-band absorption; and (ii) the slit does not encompass the entire galaxy. Using the empirical relation, we derive a lower limit to the SFR SFR > 0.3 yr^ 1

Finally, we estimate the metallicity for the galaxy associated with GRB 050709 from the measured [ N 2 ] 6583 flux relative to H using the empirical calibration given by . We measure log( [ N 2 ] /H ) = -1.3 0.1 which implies log(O/H) = 8.16 0.06 , comparable to the low metallicities observed in long-soft GRB host galaxies e.g. pro04 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 050709 host as a z=0.1606 non-early-type galaxy with young stars, ongoing star formation, blue irregular colors, and emission-line evidence.

quotes.quote: The object is coincident with a weak X-ray source and is located inside a galaxy at redshift z = 0.1606 0.0002 .
The host galaxy is not of early type. Spectra show that the dominant stellar population is relatively young ( 1 Gyr), and that ongoing star formation is present at a level of 2 - 3 L/L_* M_ /yr.

The properties of the host galaxy are intriguing. Its colours are consistent with those of an irregular galaxy at z 0.2 Fuk95 , and are much bluer than those of ellipticals (like those associated with other short GRBs). Moreover, hints of morphological structure are seen in our best-seeing images.

sources.summary: The paper treats the GRB 050709 host as an exponential-disk, irregular short-GRB host and estimates its metallicity from NII/H because OII was unavailable.

quotes.quote: Finally, for the host galaxy of GRB 050709 we lack a measurement of the OII emission line, and we thus rely on NII / H to infer 12+ log(O/H) 8.5 . The dominant source of uncertainty in this measurement is the unknown value of O_ 32 , but using a spread of a full order of magnitude results in a metallicity uncertainty of 0.2 dex.

We found that three hosts (GRBs 050709, 051221a, and 061006) are best modeled with n 1 , corresponding to an exponential disk profile, while two hosts (GRBs 050509b and 050724) are best modeled with n 3 and 5.6 , respectively, typical of elliptical galaxies.
Of the eight short GRB host galaxies only one exhibits an irregular morphology (GRB 050709) and none appear to be undergoing mergers.

gmw_00000469.name: host of GRB 050509b
gmw_00000469.overview: The host of GRB 050509b is a luminous, early-type elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0505480, arXiv:0912.4142] characterized by a de Vaucouleurs-like profile [arXiv:0912.4142] and a large effective radius [arXiv:0909.1804, arXiv:0912.4142]. It is an old galaxy with very low current star formation and no detected star formation over the past gigayear [arXiv:astro-ph/0510022].

sources.summary: The paper treats G1 as a bright elliptical galaxy aligned with the GRB 050509B error circle, models it as a lens, places its center, and finds it provides most possible magnification.

quotes.quote: The alignment of the \ \ error circle and a bright elliptical galaxy, G1, in the cluster \ makes it relevant to consider the possibility that \ is a gravitationally lensed background source es . Hence, we have calculated the gravitational lensing magnification from G1 and the cluster for several possible redshifts of .

We model the galaxy as a singular isothermal ellipsoid with an axis ratio of 0.81 bloom with the semimajor axis aligned along a position angle 90 , a velocity dispersion of 260 40 km s ^ 1 bloom , and the cluster as a singular isothermal sphere with M_ 500 =2.2_ 0.6 ^ +3.3 10^ 14 M _ (see Section 3). We place the center of the galaxy G1 at R.A.(J2000)= 12^ h 36^ m 12 86 and decl.(J2000) =+28 58 58 0 and the center of the cluster at the X-ray centroid of the diffuse cluster emission (see Section 3).

Most of the magnification is provided by the galaxy G1, and the main effect of the cluster is to increase the magnification in the East-West direction. However, if \ is positioned within 2 of G1 it could be more strongly magnified. Furthermore, bloom estimate that the probability of a chance alignment between \ and G1 is 5 10^ 3 (using the apparent magnitude of G1) while arrive at probability of 1 10^ 4 (using the luminosity and distance of G1).

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as a bright early-type elliptical near GRB 050509b, measures its cluster membership and redshift, and argues that it is likely physically associated with the burst.

quotes.quote: At 07:21:27 we highlighted the proximity of the XRT to a bright red galaxy (hereafter G1 = 2MASX J12361286+2858580) and suggested a plausible physical association bbp+05 based on its presumed membership in a z 0.22 cluster bbc+05 . We later reported the determination of the redshift in pcn+05 and pbc05 . At 08:44:13 we noted the presence of a faint, compact source (hereafter S1; see fig:lris ) in the outskirts of G1, which we deemed a plausible candidate counterpart bbp+05a .

The XRT location is 11.2 3.6 \ (or 40 13 kpc in projection) from G1 as we first noted in bbp+05 . Spectroscopy of this source reveals that it is indeed an early-type galaxy (see sec:host ) and is a member of a cluster NSC J123610+285901 at z 0.22 gcl+03,bbc+05 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies the possible GRB 050509b host as a luminous elliptical with a de Vaucouleurs-like profile and the largest effective radius in the HST host sample.

quotes.quote: The distribution of absolute rest-frame B band magnitudes ( M_B ) for these 12 host galaxies ranges from about 0.1 to 1.5 L_* ber09 , with the exception of the possible elliptical host galaxy of GRB 050509b with L_B 5 L_* bpp+06 .

We found that three hosts (GRBs 050709, 051221a, and 061006) are best modeled with n 1 , corresponding to an exponential disk profile, while two hosts (GRBs 050509b and 050724) are best modeled with n 3 and 5.6 , respectively, typical of elliptical galaxies. The final three hosts (GRBs 051210, 060121, and 060313) are equally well modeled with a wide range of n values, although n 1 is preferred. Therefore, of the eight short GRB host galaxies with HST observations only two can be robustly classified as elliptical galaxies based on their morphology.
The smallest effective radius is measured for the host of GRB 060313, while the host of GRB 050509b has the largest effective radius.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 050509b host as an old galaxy with no significant emission, very low current star formation, and no significant star formation over the past Gyr.

quotes.quote: The discovery of GRB 050509b and a fading X-ray afterglow gehrels05 led to the first redshift and host galaxy association bpp+05 for a short-hard GRB, providing unique insights for the long-standing mystery over the distance scale and energetics for at least some members of this class.

In the cases of GRB 050509b, GRB 050724, and GRB 050813, we do not detect any significant emission and we employ the SFR calibration of ken98 to place conservative upper limits on the current star formation rate (Table tab:summ ). In the host galaxies of GRB 050509b and GRB 050724, the absence of strong H absorption also indicates that there has been no significant SFR over the past 1 Gyr.
The values compare favorably with other estimates for GRB 050724 gorosabel05 , but our results contradict the young age for GRB 050509b reported by .

sources.summary: The host of GRB 050509b is modeled as an elliptical galaxy with the largest effective radius in the HST sample and a large, XRT-position-based offset.

quotes.quote: From the galfit analysis we find that three hosts (GRBs 050709, 051221a, and 061006) are best modeled with n 1 , corresponding to an exponential disk profile, while two hosts (GRBs 050509b and 050724) are best modeled with n 3 and 5.6 , respectively, typical of elliptical galaxies. We note that GRB 050724 possibly exhibits weak spiral structure, which may explain the resulting value of n 3 (see Figure  fig:050724 and mcd+07 ), but this putative spiral structure is clearly sub-dominant relative to the elliptical structure.

The smallest effective radius is measured for the host of GRB 060313, while the host of GRB 050509b has the largest effective radius. The median value is r_e 3.5 kpc. We adopt the best-fit values from the radial surface brightness profiles, and plot the resulting distribution, as well as r_e as a function of n , in Figure  fig:re_n . Finally, the effective surface brightness values range from _e 21 to 27 AB mag arcsec ^ 2 . The galaxy with the highest surface brightness is the host of GRB 050724, while the lowest surface brightness is measured for the host of GRB 060121.

Our HST sample includes three short GRBs with strong extended emission (050709, 050724, and 061006), and one GRB (060121) with possible extended emission ( 4.5 significance; dls+06 ). The physical offsets of these bursts are about 3.7, 2.7, 1.3, and 1 kpc, respectively, leading to a mean offset of about 2.2 kpc. The physical offsets of the bursts without extended emission, but with precise afterglow positions (051221, 060313, and 061201) are 2.0, 2.3, and 14.2 or 32.5 kpc, respectively. The two events with no extended emission and with XRT positions (050509b and 051210) have offsets of about 54 12 and 28 23 kpc, respectively.

gmw_00000470.name: ESO 243-49
gmw_00000470.overview: ESO 243-49 is an edge-on lenticular or early-type spiral galaxy located in the Abell 2877 cluster [arXiv:0804.4653, arXiv:1608.04238]. It features a symmetric, low-dust structure with two disk components [arXiv:0804.4653], a nuclear dust disk [arXiv:1507.02691], and an active galactic nucleus that powers its radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0001413].

sources.summary: ESO243G49 is treated as an early-type Abell 2877 spiral whose symmetric profiles imply little dust, an undisturbed edge-on orientation, and vertical structure requiring two disk components.

quotes.quote: ESO243G49 This galaxy is an early type spiral located at about 10 arcmin ( 0.3 Mpc in projected separation) from IC1633, the central dominant (cD) galaxy of Abell 2877. It has essentially the same redshift as the cluster (Abell 2877: z = 0.023 ; ESO243G49: z = 0.022 ). The R profiles shown in Figure eso243r follow the same shape with little variation from one quadrant to the next. Such symmetric appearance shows that ESO243G49 has little dust extinction and is fairly undisturbed. The symmetry also indicates that ESO243G49 is very close to a perfect edge-on orientation.

Also, nearly all these profiles deviate significantly from a straight line. This means that a single exponential disc model cannot account for the profile shapes. A two-component model is therefore necessary to successfully fit the data.

sources.summary: The paper links HLX-1 to ESO 243-49, noting UV emission extending from the galaxy nucleus toward HLX-1 and considering star formation from an interacting stripped dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: The brightest Ultra-Luminous X-ray source HLX-1 in the galaxy ESO 243-49 provides strong evidence for the existence of intermediate mass black holes. As the luminosity and thus the mass estimate depend on the association of HLX-1 with ESO 243-49, it is essential to confirm its affiliation. This requires follow-up investigations at wavelengths other than X-rays, which in-turn needs an improved source position.

At the location of the core of ESO 243-49 there is an extended object (Figure fig2 ), with some hints of an elongated emission towards the position of HLX-1. No other source is observed above the flux level of this galaxy at the Chandra position of HLX-1. We cannot determine an upper limit from subtracting the galaxy contribution at that position as we do not know when or even if HLX-1 stops emitting in the uvw2 wavelength range. A source at the X-ray position would only be discernible from the galaxy if the source is significantly brighter than the emission at that position. Therefore, to determine an upper limit to the detection of the ULX above the flux of the galaxy, we first co-added the individual observations.

sources.summary: ESO 243-49 is modeled as a high-mass edge-on S0 with an old, metal-poor thick disk whose modest lag supports rapid internal thick-disk formation.

quotes.quote: ESO 243-49 is a high-mass (circular velocity v_ 200 km s^ 1 ) edge-on S0 galaxy in the Abell 2877 cluster at a distance of 95 Mpc. To elucidate the origin of its thick disc, we use MUSE science verification data to study its kinematics and stellar populations. The thick disc emits 80 of the light at heights in excess of 3 5 (1.6 kpc). The rotation velocities of its stars lag by 30-40 km s^ 1 compared to those in the thin disc, which is compatible with the asymmetric drift.

The thick disc is found to be more metal-poor than the thin disc, but both discs have old ages. We suggest an internal origin for the thick disc stars in high-mass galaxies. We propose that the thick disc formed either a) first in a turbulent phase with a high star formation rate and that a thin disc formed shortly afterwards, or b) because of the dynamical heating of a thin pre-existing component. Either way, the star formation in ESO 243-49 was quenched just a few Gyrs after the galaxy was born and the formation of a thin and a thick disc must have occurred before the galaxy stopped forming stars.

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 243-49 as the galaxy hosting HLX-1, with a distinct nuclear radio source and UV emission possibly associated with star formation near HLX-1.

quotes.quote: The brightest Ultra-Luminous X-ray source HLX-1 in the galaxy ESO 243-49 currently provides strong evidence for the existence of intermediate mass black holes.

The brightest ultra-luminous X-ray source (ULX) currently known (HLX-1) was discovered in the second XMM-Newton Serendipitous Source Catalogue 2XMM; wat09 in the outskirts of the edge-on spiral galaxy ESO 243--49 far09 . Its 0.2 -- 10 keV unabsorbed X-ray luminosity, assuming the galaxy distance (95 Mpc), exceeded 1.1 10^ 42 ergs s ^ 1 .

A source is clearly detected coincident with the nucleus of ESO 243-49, but no source is present at the position of HLX-1. The flux density at the location of HLX-1 is 5 Jy. This is below the 3 local rms noise level of 45 Jy, which we thus adopt as the flux density upper limit for HLX-1. We fitted a Gaussian to the radio source at the nucleus of ESO 243-49, yielding an integrated flux density of 0.15 0.03 mJy. The derived position is consistent with the PDS position of RA = 01h 10m 27.69s, Dec = -46 ^ 04' 27.8'' with uncertainties of 0.2'' and 0.5'' respectively. Given the accurate astrometry in both radio and X-ray it is extremely unlikely that the 0.15 mJy source detected in the nucleus of ESO 243-49 is associated with HLX-1.

sources.summary: ESO 243-49 is used as an edge-on S0 test case, notable for HLX-1, star clusters, and a nuclear dust disk, with HST imaging modeled by the new isophote formalism.

quotes.quote: The S0 galaxy ESO 243-49 was chosen as the test-case for the new tasks and . This edge-on galaxy has been intensly studied following the discovery of a source of hyperluminous X-ray emission (referred to as HLX-1) associated with a potential intermediate-mass black hole within the galaxy but outside the nuclear region ( Farrell_etal2009 , Webb_etal2010 ). The fact that HLX-1 was found to have an optical counterpart Soria_etal2010 makes this galaxy particularly interesting to model, as one of the aims of this work is to achieve high-quality 2D galaxy models and, after subtracting them from the image data, to perform accurate photometry on the remaining substructure (such as HLX-1) left behind in the residual image. Additionally, ESO243-49 also displays a relatively large number of star clusters both in the plane of the disk and outside, as well as a dust disk in the nucleus, also viewed edge-on.

The image data for ESO 243-49 was retrieved from the Hubble Legacy Archive http://hla.stsci.edu and consisted of an H band image taken with the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3, IR channel, F160W filter, PI: S. A. Farrell) on the Hubble Space Telescope (HST). To fully showcase the improvements brought by the new formalism, the image was modelled firstly with the standard / and then with / . Both versions were run, where applicable, with identical initial conditions.

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 243-49 as the S0/a host projected around HLX-1, measuring the galaxy rotation and systemic velocity to show HLX-1 is kinematically offset.

quotes.quote: We detect an H emission line centered at = 6718.9 0.9 \ and find that its projected radial velocity with respect to the nucleus of ESO 243-49 is 424 27 km s ^ 1 , while the maximum rotational velocity of the stars in that galaxy is 209 km s ^ 1 .

HLX-1 is seen in the sky inside the D25 isophote of the S0/a galaxy ESO 243-49 [ heliocentric redshift z = 0.0224 , cosmology-corrected ade13 luminosity distance 99 Mpc, distance modulus 35.0 mag ] , at a distance of 8 3.7 kpc from the nucleus.

The rotational velocity of ESO 243-49 is 209 17 km s ^ 1 , measured as the difference between the recession velocities at =0 , and where the slit crosses the disk plane (at 20 ). With this method, we confirm a heliocentric systemic velocity 6707 16 35 km s ^ 1 for ESO 243-49, and we conclude that HLX-1 is offset from it by 424 27 km s ^ 1 ; HLX-1 is receding at a speed 215 km s ^ 1 faster than the peak stellar rotational velocity, and 270 km s ^ 1 faster than the stellar population seen projected around it in the D25 of ESO 243-49. As a further check, we estimated the rotational velocity we should expect from a galaxy such as ESO 243-49, based on the Tully-Fisher relation williams10,tully77 . The total absolute brightness (corrected for extinction and K-correction) of ESO 243-49 is M_B = -20.1 0.1 mag, M_ Ks = -24.2 0.1 mag (photometric measurements from the NED and Hyperleda databases).

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO243-49 as the edge-on S0a host of HLX-1, placing the source off the galaxy center and considering whether it originated there before receiving a recoil kick.

quotes.quote: The brightest known ULX, known as HLX-1, is located in the outskirts of the edge-on S0a type galaxy ESO243-49 and is currently the strongest IMBH candidate.

Further investigations of HLX-1 confirmed its extraordinary luminosity by proving its association with galaxy ESO243-49 at a distance of 95 Mpc WiersemaEtAl2010 , and thus made the evidence of an IMBH even stronger. Interestingly, the X-ray source is not located at the center of the host galaxy, but it lies at a distance 3.3 kpc from its center and 0.8 kpc out of the galactic plane, possibly associated with a star cluster which appears to be in the same area.
In this context, HLX-1 might be an escaping IMBH that originated at the center of ESO243-49 and received a large recoil velocity after a merger with a massive SBH.

sources.summary: The paper places HLX-1 within the D25 extent of the S0a spiral ESO 243-49, notes the redshift association, and discusses a velocity offset from the galaxy nucleus.

quotes.quote: HLX-1 is projected in the sky within the D25 (the diameter of the light isophote having a surface brightness of 25.0 mag arcsec ^ 2 ) of the S0a spiral galaxy ESO 243-49 (redshift distance 95 Mpc), 8 north-east of its nucleus Farrell2009 . The redshift measurement of the H emission line from the optical counterpart of HLX-1 confirmed the association of HLX-1 with ESO 243-49 Wiersema2010,Soria2013 . However, there is an offset of 430 km s ^ 1 between the recession velocity of HLX-1 and that of the nucleus of ESO 243-49: this suggests that HLX-1 may be in a dwarf satellite galaxy or star cluster near ESO 243-49 rather than in the galaxy itself Soria2013 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies PDF J011027.6 460428 with ESO 243 G 049, an S0 galaxy whose nuclear radio contours and absorption-line spectrum point to AGN-powered radio emission.

quotes.quote: PDF J011027.6 460428: Also catalogued as ESO 243 G 049, this S0 galaxy has radio contours centred on the optical nucleus. The spectrum shows evolved stellar population absorption features. An AGN origin for the radio emission is likely.

gmw_00000471.name: 3C 111
gmw_00000471.overview: 3C 111 is a nearby Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:0912.3850, arXiv:1207.3308] whose central component is coincident with a broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:0912.3850]. The galaxy features a highly variable, relativistic radio jet [arXiv:1207.3308, arXiv:1412.1171] and a variable ultra-fast X-ray disk outflow [arXiv:1111.0908, arXiv:1205.1734].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 111 as a nearby FR-II radio source with a broad-line Seyfert 1 central component; VERITAS observed it and found no VHE signal.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 is a near ( z=0.0485 ) FR-II radio source whose central component is coincident with a broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy. The radio morphology shows a double-lobe/single-jet structure with the jet emerging at an angle of 63^ . The central component is variable on time scales of a few months. Hints of superluminal behaviour are observed .

VERITAS observed the radio galaxy 3C 111 during fall 2008 at a zenith angle range between 15^ and 30^ . All data taken under bad weather conditions or with technical problems have been discharged. Finally, a total amount of about 11 hours has remained for analysis purposes. No VHE signal has been detected, a 99 confidence level upper limit above the analysis threshold of 300 GeV has been derived.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 111 as a Seyfert 1 with episodic millimeter outbursts, mostly quiescent fluxes, asymmetric flux distributions, and simultaneous 1.3-mm and 3-mm lightcurves.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 This Seyfert 1 galaxy shows episodic strong variability on timescales of years. The source seems to spend most of the time in a low-activity or ``quiescent'' state with fluxes in the range 1--3 Jy. In addition, we can identify at least five pronounced spikes in the lightcurves in 1997, 2001, 2006, 2008, and 2009. These outbursts reach flux densities of 6--15 Jy, thus exceeding the quiescent level by factors 3--6.

The 3-mm (1.3-mm) flux distributions can be described by smooth, asymmetric profiles composed of two Gaussian components for fluxes 8 Jy ( 5 Jy) plus sparsely populated tails ranging out to 14 Jy ( 10 Jy). Except for the tails, it is also possible to describe the histograms with (unimodal) log-normal distributions.
Although this is a relatively high value, the log-normal distribution appears to be an acceptable description of the data. Within errors, the 1.3-mm and 3-mm lightcurves are simultaneous.

sources.summary: Suzaku follow-up of 3C 111 monitored a variable ultra-fast outflow, linking blue-shifted Fe absorption to disk winds with substantial mechanical power and AGN feedback potential.

quotes.quote: The bright BLRG 3C 111 was proposed by us in the Suzaku GO5 as the best candidate for a follow-up study of the predicted 7 days time-scale variability of its UFO (Tombesi et al. 2011b). It is important to note that 3C 111, as well as 3C 120, have been recently the subjects of an extensive monitoring campaign to study their accretion disk-jet connection (Chatterjee et al. 2009, 2011). The three Suzaku observations have exposures of 60 ks and there is a 30 flux increase between the first and the second.

For the latter, instead, we find that assuming the likely identification with blue-shifted Fe XXV--XXVI absorption lines, a photo-ionization modeling suggest they are due to a highly ionoized, log 4.3 erg s ^ 1 cm, UFO with outflow velocity 0.1c and column density N_H 8 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 . The location of this material is constrained at <0.006 pc from the black hole, which is consistent with accretion disk winds/outflows. The mass outflow rate is comparable to the accretion rate of 1 M_ yr ^ 1 and the mechanical power is 5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , which is comparable to the typical jet power. This latter corresponds also to about half of the X-ray luminosity and 6 of the bolometric luminosity. Therefore, this outflow constitutes a non negligible fraction of the overall energy budget of the AGN and can potentially provide a significant feedback effect besides the jet.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 111 as an FRII broad-line radio galaxy whose LAT detection, radio-knot ejection, and compact gamma-ray source localize emission to the radio-core jet.

quotes.quote: 3C 111(z=0.0491) is a BLRG exhibiting radio lobes, a strong core and a knotty jet. It is a source extensively monitored from radio to X-rays by the blazar research group at Boston University (BU) www.bu.edu/blazars/ . 3C 111 is listed in the first but not in the second LAT Catalog of AGN .

The Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) images of 3C 111 at three different epochs of 2008 is shown in Figure 3. The radio core is weak in August, very bright in November and split into two components in December. This jet modification roughly follows the ray flux evolution, revealing a direct connection between the ray outburst and the ejection of a new radio knot. This unambiguously localizes the -ray source in the radio core.
The compact ray source of the FRII RG 3C 111, being at 0.3 pc from the central engine, is then necessarily confined within the torus.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 111 as an FR-II radio galaxy with Seyfert-like thermal X-ray features, variable nonthermal jet emission, and gamma-rays attributed to its jet.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 is a FR-II radio galaxy at redshift z = 0.049 ( D = 205 Mpc ), which displays a broad line region in the optical and iron K emission in the X-rays, similar to Seyfert galaxies. The radio jet has an angle of 18^ to the line of sight. This object has been first detected by Fermi /LAT in the gamma-rays, with a photon index of = 2.4 0.2 in the range 0.1 - 200 GeV and a flux of f 10^ 8 ph cm^ 2 s^ 1 .

This seems to suggest that although there is compelling evidence for Seyfert-like emission from 3C 111, the X-rays are most likely also influenced by non-thermal jet emission. This is further supported by the fact that the equivalent width of the iron line and the continuum emission are variable, and we observe a highly-significant anti-correlation between the equivalent width and the continuum flux. It seems, that the variations of the underlying continuum by a factor of 4 are driven by the variable non-thermal emission, while the Seyfert core remains persistent. Thus, for 3C 111 we conclude that the X-rays show contribution of both, non-thermal jet emission and thermal inverse Compton emission similar to that in Seyfert galaxies. Also here, like in the other radio galaxies, we assume that the gamma-ray emission can be entirely attributed to jet emission, because the thermal IC component with its exponential cut-off in the hard X-rays will not contribute significantly beyond 500 keV .

sources.summary: The paper images radio galaxy 3C 111 at multiple epochs, finding a northeast jet, knotty 43 GHz features, and superluminal component motions around 4.6c to 5.2c.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 As for BL Lac, we obtained four images of 3C 111 in total (Figure fig:fig10 ). 3C 111 is a radio galaxy with a redshift of 0.491 Eracleous2004 . The corresponding angular scale is 0.95 pc/mas. A long jet, which extends up to 10 mas in north-east direction, can be found in each image. In the 43 GHz maps several knotty features are visible which are not distinguishable at 22 GHz.

3C 111 Figure fig:fig20 shows the comparison between observations of 3C 111 obtained in 2014 and 2015. At 22 GHz, the time gap between the two observations is 161 days. Components J0--J4 moved between 0.31 and 0.76 parsec, corresponding to a (average) apparent speed of around 4.6c (Table tab:table11 ). Apparent speeds range from 2.9c to 5.2c .

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 111 jet knots under IC/CMB emission, identifying K30 as the brightest hard-X-ray knot and a potential CTAO VHE test case.

quotes.quote: Among the two sources predicted as a CTAO VHE candidate under IC/CMB model, 3C 111 belongs to FRII morphology 1974MNRAS.167P..31F located at z=0.049. The radio observation suggests a 100 kilo-parsec long jet and Chandra was able to resolve nearly 10 knots with at least eight which are prominent in X-rays 2016ApJ...826..109C . Among them, the knots K 22, K 30 and K 61 indicate a hard X-ray spectrum suggesting an inverse Compton origin of the X-ray emission. Whereas, the knots K 9, K 14, K 38, K 45, K 51, K 97 and K 107 has steeper X-ray spectrum supporting a synchrotron origin 2018ApJ...858...27Z .

Hence, for the present work we have considered only the three X-ray knots K 30, K 61 and K 22, with K 30 being the brightest among them. Modelling the X-ray emission as IC/CMB mechanism predicts significant VHE flux for the knot K 30 which can be examined by future CTAO observations. The predicted VHE flux of K 61 and K 22 fall below the CTAO sensitivity though the X-ray flux of K 61 is comparable with K 30. We anticipate the reason for this being the steep X-ray spectrum of K 61 as compared to K 30.

sources.summary: The paper reports ROSAT observations of 3C 111, a powerful broad-line radio galaxy with optical hot spots, detecting a compact nuclear X-ray source, non-detected hot spots, and variable X-ray behavior.

quotes.quote: We report on ROSAT HRI observations of the nearby powerful radio galaxies 3C 33 and 3C 111, which both have detected optical hot spots. We find nuclear X-ray sources in both objects, but no X-ray emission from the hot spots. This confirms the presence of a high-energy cutoff in the spectrum of synchrotron-emitting electrons.

3C111 3C 111 was observed with the HRI for a total of 12.9 ks between 1996 September 15 and 17. The data were reduced in a similar way to those from 3C 33, and after high-background time filtering there were 10.5 ks of good data. Fig. \ 3C111-pic shows an overlay of the radio contours of Leahy \ (1997) on the X-ray image. A strong source was detected at a J2000 position of 04 18 21.3 +38 01 38.5, approximately 2.5 arcsec away from the radio core position of Linfield Perley (1984). Using source and background regions identical in size to those used for 3C 33, the count rate for the source is 0.073 s ^ 1 , or 760 30 counts in total.

sources.summary: The paper gives millimeter polarimetric monitoring results for 0415+379, including detection frequency, polarization behavior, flux evolution, variability, and rotation-measure constraints.

quotes.quote: 0415+379 (3C 111) For this Seyfert 1 galaxy located at z 0.05 , 17 out of 34 polarimetric observations, obtained between March 2007 and January 2010, detected linearly polarized emission. The average degree of polarization is m_L 2.3 . Initially, low polarization levels below 2 and polarization angles around 0^ (modulo 0^ /180^ ambiguities) are predominant. In August 2007, the polarization changed to m_L 4-6 and 100^ . After April 2008, polarization could only be detected at one occasion (April 2009, m_L 1 , 120^ ). The degree of polarization fluctuates at (normalized) rates of _m 0.09 d ^ 1 and _m/ m_L 0.04 d ^ 1 , respectively.

The 3-mm flux density was highly variable, with the average value being S_ 7.5 Jy. In mid-2007, flux measurements commenced at values S_ 10 Jy, rising to S_ 15 Jy shortly thereafter. Since then, the flux declined to S_ 2 Jy, with a short-lived intermediate high of S_ 7 Jy observed at the end of 2008.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 111 as a nearby FRII radio galaxy detected by Fermi-LAT, with Suzaku and XMM spectra showing variable flux and iron K emission.

quotes.quote: A typical FRII: 3C 111 3C 111 (z=0.0491) is a nearby FRII radio galaxy located at low Galactic latitude. Together with Centaurus A and NGC 6251, they are the only radio galaxies considered as EGRET candidate sources - . The Fermi -LAT instrument detected a -ray flare from this source in the period 2008 October--November - , immediately after an X--ray flux minimum (see Figure lc_x ). Here we present the analysis of two X--ray observations performed by Suzaku (t _ exp 122 ks) and XMM--Newton (t _ exp 124 ks) on 2008 August 22 and 2009 February 15, respectively.

During the XMM--Newton observation the flux of 3C 111 was higher by a factor 2.5 (see Figures lc_x - suz_xmm ). The spectrum is again well fitted by a power law ( 1.6) absorbed by Galactic N _ H similar to that measured in the previous observation. Residuals to the power law model reveal the presence of the iron K line at E=6.4 0.04 keV, characterized by =0.1 0.06 keV and EW=67 ^ +19 _ 23 eV In both Suzaku and XMM--Newton spectra the addition of a narrow Fe K component around 7 keV further improves the fit.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 111 broad-band X-ray spectra, describing it as a powerful radio galaxy with jet, reflection, Fe lines, and sub-pc outflow/jet feedback connections.

quotes.quote: We present a combined Suzaku and Swift BAT broad-band E = 0.6--200 keV spectral analysis of three 3C 111 observations obtained in 2010. The data are well described with an absorbed power-law continuum and a weak ( R 0.2 ) cold reflection component from distant material. We constrain the continuum cutoff at E_ C 150--200 keV, which is in accordance with X-ray Comptonization corona models and supports claims that the jet emission is only dominant at much higher energies.

3C 111 is a powerful and X-ray bright radio galaxy at z =0.0485 with a SMBH mass of log M_ BH = 8.1 0.5 M_ (Chatterjee et al. 2011). The inclination of the radio jet is = 18^ 5^ (Jorstad et al. 2005) or 10 ^ < < 26^ (Lewis et al. 2005). The source has been recently the subject of an extensive monitoring campaign to study its accretion disc-jet connection (Chatterjee et al. 2011). Indeed, major X-ray dips in the light curve are followed by ejections of bright superluminal knots in the radio jet.

sources.summary: The paper maps 3C 111 at four radio frequencies, resolving three components, spectral information, and a polarization peak offset from the total-intensity core.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 We obtained total intensity maps of the radio galaxy 3C 111 at all four observing frequencies (Figure fig:3c111_imaps ). 3C 111 consists of three components in all maps. For two frequency pairs (22/43 and 86/129 GHz) we were able to cross-identify components and obtained spectral information.

Figure fig:3c111_polmaps displays the polarization structure of the source. The level of polarization is on the order of a few per cent. Interestingly, the peak of the polarized intensity is offset from the total intensity core. This may be caused by larger depolarization in the core due to higher opacity or by an unresolved strongly polarized jet component.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Chandra and XMM spectra of the BLRG 3C 111, classifying it as an FRII radio galaxy and attributing its excess absorption to Galactic Taurus-cloud gas rather than intrinsic material.

quotes.quote: The radio galaxy 3C 111 is an X-ray bright BLRG and it is classified as a Fanaroff--Riley type II (FRII) source with a double-lobe/single-jet morphology (Linfield Perley 1984). The inner jet has been seen to display superluminal motion (Vermeulen Cohen 1994; Chatterjee et al. 2011). This source was also detected in the ray band with Fermi (Kataoka et al. 2011; Grandi et al. 2012).

The analysis of the Chandra HETG and XMM-Newton RGS spectra of 3C 111 allowed us to constrain the excess absorption as due to intervening gas in the Milky Way and to estimate a total time-averaged column density of N_H = (7.4 0.1) 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 . What is the origin of the extra Galactic neutral absorption of N_H = 4.4 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 with respect to the atomic H I value? 3C 111 is located at a relatively low latitude (b = 8.8^ ) with respect to the Galactic plane and it is known to lie behind the giant Taurus molecular cloud, which is the nearest large star-forming region in our Galaxy, at an estimated distance of about 200 pc from us (Ungerer et al. 1985; Ungerechts Thaddeus 1987).

We also detect a weak (EW = 16 10 eV) and narrow (FWMH < 5,500 km s ^ 1 , consistent with optical H ) Fe K emission line at E = 6.4 keV likely from the torus in the central regions of 3C 111, and we place an upper limit on the column density of a possible intrinsic warm absorber of N_H < 2.5 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 . These complexities make 3C 111 a very promising object for studying both the intrinsic properties of this active radio galaxy and the Galactic interstellar medium if used as a background source.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLBI polarization imaging of 3C111, describing it as a nearby FR II radio galaxy with a compact core, aligned jets, evolving knots, and Faraday or optical-depth effects.

quotes.quote: Galaxies: Individual (3C111) Interferometry Polarimetry The broad-line radio galaxy 3C111 (0415+739) is the nearest ( z=0.0485 ) classical FR II radio galaxy with a strong compact core at cm/mm wavelengths Wills75 . Radio observations on kiloparsec scales show a highly collimated jet leading from the core to the northeastern lobe LinfieldPerley84 . On parsec scales, the jet is much more prominent and is one-sided towards the northeast, roughly aligned with the kpc-scale jet Linfield81 .

Our images of 3C111 for two epochs separated by only 2.5 months reveal dramatic changes in the VLBI structure after a millimetre outburst. The 43-GHz images show obvious expansion of a bright knot from the core.

Comparison of our 43 and 8-GHz images for July 1996 shows a large offset between the P vectors at 0.5-1 mas, indicating that Faraday and/or optical-depth effects may be playing a significant role. However, these images also suggest that the P vectors in the core region have nearly the same orientations at the two frequencies, in which case Faraday and optical depths are less important there than in the inner jet.

sources.summary: The paper compares ultra-fast X-ray disk outflows with the radio jet of the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 111, arguing that the components coexist on sub-pc scales and may jointly affect feedback.

quotes.quote: We present a comparison of the parameters of accretion disc outflows and the jet of the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 111 on sub-pc scales. We make use of published X-ray observations of ultra-fast outflows (UFOs) and new 43 GHz VLBA images to track the jet knots ejection. We find that the superluminal jet coexists with the mildly relativistic outflows on sub-pc scales, possibly indicating a transverse stratification of a global flow. The two are roughly in pressure equilibrium, with the UFOs potentially providing additional support for the initial jet collimation.

The UFOs are much more massive than the jet, but their kinetic power is probably about an order of magnitude lower, at least for the observations considered here. However, their momentum flux is equivalent and both of them are powerful enough to exert a concurrent feedback impact on the surrounding environment.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 111’s X-ray-to-gamma-ray emission, concluding that thermal Seyfert-like X-rays and non-thermal jet processes together explain its broadband output.

quotes.quote: We attempt to determine the nature of the high energy emission of the radio galaxy 3C 111, by distinguishing between the effects of the thermal and non-thermal processes. We study the X-ray spectrum of 3C 111 between 0.4 keV and 200 keV, and its spectral energy distribution, using data from the Suzaku satellite combined with INTEGRAL, Swift/BAT data, and Fermi/LAT data. We then model the overall spectral energy distribution by including radio and infrared data.

3C 111 is a nearby z=0.049, Sargent1977 flat-spectrum FR-II radio galaxy Fanaroff1974, Linfield1984 that displays strong, broad emission-lines in the optical Sargent1977 and an iron-emission line in the X-ray regime Lewis2005 , similar to Seyfert galaxies. In the radio regime, the galaxy's emission is dominated by the relativistic jet, which has an angle of 18 to our line of sight Jorstad2005 . The projected size of the jet is 78 kpc Bridle1984 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 111 as a nearby source with a detected jet-shape break, fits its data with maximum Lorentz factors 10 and 20, and derives low M2 spin estimates.

quotes.quote: Our sample of 11 sources include 3C 111, 3C 120 and BL Lac with the earlier detected jet boundary shape break Kovalev20_r1 . This detection was possible due to the sources proximity (redshift <0.07 ), which provided the necessary linear resolution. Our aim is to check the consistency of the analytical and semi-analytical modelling for these sources, data on the value of a Lorentz factor and the additional data on the parabolic jet shape.

We are able to fit the data for 3C 111 and 3C 120 with _ max =10, 20 . The models with higher _ max start further downstream, so applying these models would require extrapolating the analytical curves upstream. However, we observe, that fitting the particular point in z -- plane provides smaller spin for larger _ max (unlike fitting the jet shape break in NKP20_r2 ), so taking smaller _ max we model larger BH spin values.

sources.summary: 3C 111 is described as a luminous FR II radio galaxy with a bright core, one-sided milli-arcsecond jet, asymmetric double radio structure, possible superluminal motion, and weak Compton reflection.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 3C 111 is a luminous FR II radio galaxy with bright core, a one-sided jet on milli-arcsecond scale (Linfield Perley 1984), a highly asymmetric double radio structure, and reported superluminal motion ( 10 , Preuss, Alef Kellermann 1988). The optical image shows no structure (Colina P erez-Fournon 1990) and thus exact classification is not possible, but the properties of the host galaxy are consistent with elliptical morphology. The spectrum of 3C 111 from was included in the sample of NP94. The parameters of our fits to the observation are reported in Table 6 and the spectrum is shown in Figure 4.
The remaining fit parameters are similar to those of NP94, in particular there is little evidence for reflection.
This means that the line emission from 3C 111 can originate from Compton reflection, but the /2 0.3 implies the reflector geometry is different from a slab.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 111 as a nearby broad-line FR II radio galaxy with complex continuum structure, including a core, two lobes with hotspots, and MERLIN-detected compact emission.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 The large low latitude source 3C 111 ( l = 161 68, b = --8 82) has a complex continuum structure. This FR (Fanaroff-Riley) II source is a nearby broad line radio galaxy at a redshift of 0.049. VLA continuum studies were carried out by lp84 ; gl86 have also summarized VLA observations of this source, identifying a core continuum component (their `2' ) and two prominent lobes with hotspots, NE (their `1', displacement 2 arcmin) and SW (their `3' and `4', displaced by 80 arcsec). Using the MERLIN array, the NE component is detected with an extent of 2.5 arcsec and a continuum peak of only 46 mJy beam ^ 1 (not listed in Table obspar ); the mean spectrum was obtained after smoothing to a 0.3-arcsec resolution. The core of the galaxy has a peak of 1.27 Jy beam ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 111 as a radio-loud AGN with possible intervening absorption, weak iron-line evidence, significant reflection, and a roughly 100 keV cut-off.

quotes.quote: Note, however, that in 3C 111 the measured absorption could be due to a molecular cloud located between us and the source b31 and not intrinsic to the AGN. In two sources, namely 3C 111 and IGR J21247+5058, the equivalent width values are close to the capability limits of moderate resolution CCD instruments like XMM-Newton , calling for some caution in distinguishing between a real feature or just local noise. 3C 111 and IGR J21247+5058; the cut-off is localised at around 100 keV for 3C 111 and 80 keV for IGR J21247+5058.

The reflection component is also significantly required (at more than 99 confidence level) in 3C 111 and IGR J21247+5058, the two AGN which also have a high energy cut-off; since in these two objects the iron line is very weak or possibly even absent we expect to observe negligible or low reflection and we thus suspect that a better fit is achieved when both R and E _ cut are left free to vary (see next section). In the case of 3C 111, all observations point to a small reflection and a compatibly low iron line EW b7,b33 ; our study yields a slightly higher value of R which is however still compatible, within errors, with previous results.

sources.summary: The paper simulates 3C 111 as a broad-line radio galaxy with UFO absorption, a variable radio jet, gamma-ray detection, and disk-jet-outflow relevance.

quotes.quote: 3C 111 We performed a 100 ks realistic ASTRO-H calorimeter simulation of the broad-line radio galaxy 3C 111 based on the 100 ks Suzaku XIS spectrum taken in 2008. We focused only in the E = 4--10 keV band. Blue-shifted Fe K absorption lines were detected in this spectrum by tombesi10b and gofford12 , indicating that this is one of the few radio-loud AGN showing UFOs to date. Interestingly, this source shows also a powerful, time variable relativistic radio jet and was detected in the rays with the Fermi satellite. Therefore, the study of its UFO has important implications for the investigation of the possible connection between the disk, the jet and outflows and their feedback on the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents deep Chandra and HST observations of the 3C 111 jet, finding near-IR and X-ray jet components, hotspots, band-dependent morphology, and evidence against EC/CMB emission.

quotes.quote: Here we discuss a spectacular example of this class, the 3C 111 jet, housed in one of the nearest, double-lobed FR II radio galaxies known. We discuss new, deep Chandra and HST observations that reveal both near-IR and X-ray emission from several components of the 3C 111 jet, as well as both the northern and southern hotspots. Important differences are seen between the morphologies in the radio, X-ray, and near-IR bands. The long (over 100 kpc on each side), straight nature of this jet makes it an excellent prototype for future, deep observations, as it is one of the longest such features seen in the radio, near-IR/optical and X-ray bands. Several independent lines of evidence, including the X-ray and broadband spectral shape as well as the implied velocity of the approaching hotspot, lead us to strongly disfavor the EC/CMB model and instead favor a two-component synchrotron model to explain the observed X-ray emission for several jet components.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 111 as a typical FR II radio galaxy with a massive black hole, known distance, three northern-jet knots, and VLBA jet constraints.

quotes.quote: 3C 111: 3C 111 is a typical FR II radio galaxy with a SMBH of mass 2.0 10^ 8 Ito2021 . It is located at a redshift of z = 0.158, corresponding to a luminosity distance of 215 Mpc. \ observations by Clautice16a report emission from three knots, K14, K30, and K61 in the northern jet. VLBA observations reveal an angle to the line of sight 20 and a velocity 0.98 for the entire jet Oh2015 . The radio and optical data are taken from Clautice16a , and the data are taken from Zhang2018a .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 111 as an AGN with a Suzaku Fe-K absorption feature modeled as an ultra-fast outflow, including its ionization and velocity in the wind analysis.

quotes.quote: used the Suzaku X-ray Imaging Spectrometer (XIS) to observe 3C 111, an AGN at z = 0.0485. Of the three observations in their study, one showed evidence of an absorption line in the Fe K band. This observation had an exposure of 59 ksec. Using XSTAR grids with turbulent velocity of 3000 km s ^ 1 , this one feature was fit with an ionization parameter of = 4.32 0.12 and an outflowing velocity of v_ out = 0.106 0.006 .

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 111 as the radio-galaxy case where X-ray UFOs and VLBA radio jets coexist, with model wind pressures and kinetic power matching the observed jet/UFO environment.

quotes.quote: With little simultaneous X-ray-radio studies having been conducted so far in radio-loud AGNs, hereafter T12b Tombesi12b did perform such a combined study of the X-ray UFOs and the VLBA radio jet of the radio galaxy 3C 111; their simultaneous presence in this object has indicated that these components can actually coexist in AGN.

We have argued further that the same winds are also compatible with the more recently discovered UFOs and that in fact these features are different facets of the same underlying phenomenon, which apparently also occurs in radio galaxies, like 3C 111. We have also argued that the radio/X-ray observations of this later object imply pressure equilibrium between its radio jet emitting plasma and the plasma associated with its UFOs, suggesting that the former occupies, in pressure equilibrium, the near-axis region of the self-similar UFO-WA flows of our models.

Assuming r_ in /r_o 100 this yields E _ out ^ (global) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 that is indeed consistent with the estimated UFO power in 3C 111 ( Tombesi10b,Tombesi12a , T12b) providing a large impact on AGN feedback process at large scales.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 0415+379 (3C111) has weak core polarization but a clear transverse RM gradient across the jet about 6 mas from the core.

quotes.quote: 0415+379 (3C111). The RM maps for this source are shown in Fig. fig:rmmaps-ZT (second row). The polarization in the core and innermost jet is weak, leading to the measurement of RM values only in the jet, well separated from the core region. Both the RM maps made using the elliptical (left panel) and equal-area circular (middle panel) beams show a clear, monotonic transverse RM gradient across the jet approximately 6 mas from the core. The gradient has significances of 3.7 and about 6 in the elliptical-beam and circular-beam RM maps, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 111 as an AGN whose 73-402 day X-ray dip timescales are consistent with hot inner accretion-disc collapse timescales.

quotes.quote: We construct simplified disc models using this viscosity prescription and estimate disc collapse timescales for 3C 120, 3C 111, and GRS 1915+105. The Shakura-Sunyaev parameter resulting from our model ranges from 0.02 to 0.08. Our inner disc collapse timescale estimates agree well with those of the observed X-ray dips.
The dip timescales for 3C 111 are in the range 73--402 days.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 111 as a broad-line FR II radio galaxy with a one-sided jet, an EGRET association, and a quasar-like broadband SED profile.

quotes.quote: B0415 + 379 3C 111 is a well-studied broad-line radio galaxy which shows a classical Fanaroff and Riley Class II morphology on kiloparsec scales linfield84 . It hosts a highly collimated one-sided jet emitting from the central core to the northeastern lobe (see e.g., kadler08 ). 3C 111 is associated with the EGRET source 3EG J0416 + 3650, and the broadband SED study of the historical data suggests that the SED profile is similar to EGRET flat spectrum radio quasars hartman08 .

sources.summary: 3C 111 is described as the FR II exception in the sample and as a Seyfert-like HERG whose X-ray spectrum is dominated by disk/corona emission.

quotes.quote: All these radio galaxies are of the FR I type, with the exception of 3C 111 and NGC 6251 which display classical FR II and intermediate FR I/FR II large-scale radio morphologies, respectively e.g., gli04,sam04,gra12 .
Among our sample of targets, four HERGs including Cen A, NGC 1275, 3C 120, and 3C 111 reveal clear Fe-K lines, while the objects classified as LERGs --- namely M 87, , , and NGC 6251 --- do not.

This finding can be considered as further support for the scenario for the disk/corona emission dominating the X-ray spectra in ``Seyfert-like'' sources NGC 1275, 3C 111, and 3C 120, and the jet emission dominating the X-ray output of the outliers like .

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 111 as a luminous FR-II comparison, citing its higher Doppler factor, smaller emitting region, larger magnetic field, and stronger luminosity relative to M 87.

quotes.quote: Comparing the SED parameters with the luminous ( L_ bol =5 10^ 44 erg s^ 1 ) FR-II galaxy 3C 111 shows the differences between these two types of sources. A larger Doppler factor =14 , a smaller emitting region ( R=2 10^ 16 cm ) and larger magnetic field ( B= 0.04 G ) are used to model 3C 111 deJong2012 . The smaller volume of the emitting region used to model 3C 111 compared to M 87 yields a lower flux, but the strong Doppler factor and larger magnetic field of 3C 111 increase the flux strongly, consistent with the larger bolometric luminosity of this source compared to M 87.

sources.summary: The review describes 3C 111 as an FR II broad-line radio galaxy where a 2008 multiwavelength outburst was tied to a compact radio-core blob ejection.

quotes.quote: In order to establish where the high-energy radiation is emitted a multi-wavelength (MW) approach is necessary, as demonstrated by the successful results obtained for the broad-line radio galaxies 3C 111 and 3C 120 3C 111 is a FR II, while 3C 120 although morphologically classified as FR I, is characterised by a powerful accretion disk. Interestingly, these are the only AGN for which a disk/jet connection has been established ( , ).

In 3C 111, a MW outburst occurred in late 2008 (Fig. fig1 right panel ) was attributed to the ejection of a new radio blob ( ): the emitting region was compact ( 0.1 pc) and the event was localised in the radio core at 0.3 pc from the black hole.

sources.summary: The paper includes 3C 111 in its strong UHECR source subset and says canonical boosting makes it a substantial contributor to the modeled UHECR flux and dipole direction.

quotes.quote: We particularly note that 6 objects out of this list of 16 --- Pic A, 3C 111, Cen B, PKS 0521-36, PKS 1610-60 and PKS 1814-63 --- are not contained in vV12. This figure shows that besides the ``usual suspects'' Cen A (310,+19) , Vir A (284,-74) , For A (240,-57) and Cyg A (76,+6) , for the case of canonical boosting also Per A (151,-13) , 3C 111 (162,-9) , PKS 0521-36 (241,-33) and PKS 1814-63 (331,-21) are expected to contribute to the UHECR flux on a similar level.

We see that for > 8 EeV the direction of its total dipole is in excellent agreement with Auger results, mostly caused by the strong contributions of 3C 111, Per A and PKS 0521-36, and still in reasonable agreement for > 32 EeV.

gmw_00000472.name: Tol1326-379
gmw_00000472.overview: Tol1326-379 is a Fanaroff-Riley type 0 radio galaxy [arXiv:2112.07958] that has been associated with a Fermi-LAT gamma-ray source [arXiv:1512.01242, arXiv:1909.04113], though this spatial association remains questioned [arXiv:2112.07958, arXiv:2307.08379]. Its core spectral energy distribution displays a double-humped shape [arXiv:1711.03757, arXiv:1909.04113] dominated by non-thermal jet emission [arXiv:1711.03757, arXiv:2307.08379].

sources.summary: The paper models Tol 1326-379 as a LAT-associated benchmark FR0 radiogalaxy whose double-humped SED implies a compact jet, while cautioning it may not represent all FR0 sources.

quotes.quote: Grandi et al. (2016) associate the FR0 radiogalaxy Tol 1326-379 to a ray source detected by LAT (3FGLJ1330.0--818). Its spectral energy distribution (SED), assembled with the available data, is shown in Fig. sed . The ``double-humped" shape closely resemble that displayed by other jet-dominated radio-loud AGNs, blazars and radiogalaxies, for which the bulk of the non-thermal emission is thought to be produced in the part of the jet close to the central engine. This evidence strongly supports the view that, at small scales ( few pc), the jet hosted by FR0 is similar to those residing in the classical radio-loud objects. As discussed in Grandi et al. (2016), the data points in the optical-UV band are likely contaminated by optical emission from the galaxy and should be formally treated as upper limits to the jet emission. On the other hand, the IR datapoint (from WISE ) is probably associated to the non-thermal jet emission.

In the following we assume these figures derived for Tol 1326-379 as a benchmark to discuss the implications for the production of neutrinos. As a caveat , we note that the scope of this exercise is necessarily limited, since the properties of the FR0 are far from clear and we are not sure that Tol 1326-379 can be really considered a representative case for the overall FR0 population. An analysis similar to that performed for Tol 1326-379 extended to other FR0 could be useful to better assess the properties of these sources.

sources.summary: Tol 1326 379 is the lone Fermi-associated FR 0 in the review, with lower GeV luminosity than blazars, a double-humped core SED, synchrotron-dominated low energies, and SSC-compatible modeling.

quotes.quote: Currently, only one FR 0 has been associated with a ray counterpart by Fermi , i.e., Tol 1326 379 . The source is characterized by a GeV luminosity (L _ >1 GeV = 2 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 ) lower than blazars, consistent with FR Is but with a slightly steeper photon index ( _ = 2.8) than classical FR Is. The radio-GeV spectral energy distribution (SED) of the core is double-humped (see Figure FR0SED a) similar to other jet-dominated radio-loud AGN. Despite the similar radio luminosity, M 87 is less luminous than Tol 1326 379 by a factor of 30 at 1 GeV and has a flatter SED in the ray domain. In contrast, the SEDs of CenA and Tol 1326 379 are quite similar in shape with a steep ray trend, but the former source is about two orders of magnitude fainter.

At low energies, the non-thermal synchrotron component of Tol 1326 379 dominates over any accretion-related contribution, further supporting the case of an ADAF disc suggested by the X-ray study (see and Section sec2.1 ). The peak at the high-energies (so-called Compton peak) appears to be relatively prominent, comparable with or even higher than the synchrotron one, differently from BL Lac sources. The whole SED can be consistently reproduced by a synchrotron and synchrotron-self-Compton (SSC) model assuming either an aligned ( _ v < 1/ _ jet ) or a misaligned ( _ v 30 ^ , ) jet, with a total jet energy flux of the order of few 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper examines whether TOL 1326--379 is associated with a Fermi/LAT gamma-ray source, finding an ambiguous positional association but SED behavior similar to gamma-ray-emitting radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: With the possible spacial association to the Fermi/LAT source 3FGL J1330.0--3818, TOL 1326--379 may be the first one that is identified as a ray emitting Fanaroff--Riley type 0 radio galaxy (FR0 RG). We analyze the 12 yr Fermi/LAT observation data of this ray source and examine its association to TOL 1326--379. We show that the ray source (named as J1331.0--3818) is tentatively detected with a TS value of 28.7, 3FGL J1330.0--3818 is out of the 95 containment of J1331.0--3818, and their positions are spatially separated 0.2 . 4FGL J1331.3--3818 falls into the 68 containment of J1331.0--3818, suggesting that our result agrees with that reported in the Fourth Fermi LAT Source Catalog. TOL 1326--379 is out of the 95 containment of J1331.0--3818, and their positions are spatially separated 0.4 , indicating that the association between J1331.0--3818 and TOL 1326--379 is quite ambiguous.

The spectral energy distribution (SED) of TOL 1326--379 shows a bimodal feature as seen in the ray emitting RGs. We fit the SED with the one-zone leptonic model and find that the average energy spectrum of J1331.0--3818 agrees with the model prediction. Assuming that J1331.0--3818 is an unidentified ray source, we derive the upper-limit of the ray flux for TOL 1326--379.

sources.summary: The paper associates Tol1326-379 with a Fermi source, describes it as an FR 0 radio galaxy, and argues its gamma-ray SED and steep spectrum constrain jet orientation and structure.

quotes.quote: We present supporting evidence for the first association of a Fermi source, 3FGLJ1330.0-3818, with the FR 0 radio galaxy Tol1326-379.

Tol1326-379 has a GeV luminosity of L_ >1 GeV 2 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 , typical of FR Is, but with a steeper ray spectrum ( =2.78 0.14 ). This could be related to the intrinsic jet properties but also to a different viewing angle.

Although we do not have any direct estimate for the orientation of Tol1326-379, we speculate that it is oriented at a large angle and this causes the similarity between its SED and that of Cen A. Alternatively, if we are seeing the jet of Tol1326-379 at a small angle, its SED would be intrinsically different from those of the FR Is. This would be the first indication of a discrepant property between these two classes (other than the paucity of extended radio emission in FR 0) and might be an important clue to understand their nature. Interesting enough, the Compton peak in Tol1326-379 appears to be more prominent than the Synchrotron one.
